***A Vindication of the Rights of Woman: with Strictures on Political and Moral Subjects*** is a 1792 essay by British philosopher and women's rights advocate Mary Wollstonecraft . It is considered a feminist or, more precisely, proto-feminist piece, and one of the pioneering works in the tradition now known as feminist philosophy .

title: Vindicating the Feminism of Mary Wollstonecraft
excerpts:
feminist authoress Mary Wollstonecraft (1759–1797) is most often
remembered by contemporary scholars for her works *Vindication*
*of the Rights of Men* (1790) and *Vindication of the Rights of Woman* (1792).
Through these pieces, Wollstonecraft comprehensively and earnestly
addresses controversial issues surrounding class and gender in the iconic
voice of rebellion that earned her recognition and fame, even in her own
time. Due to the scope of her social concerns, and because of her unique
method of argumentation, Wollstonecraft is studied today in a variety
of academic disciplines. Her use of Christian reasoning appeals to schol -
ars of religion. Her direct contribution to an ongoing and ever-evolving
feminist movement attracts attention from scholars working in the field
of Women’s Studies. Academics of English literature study her use of lan-
guage and her conceptualization techniques, and the field of philosophy

title: Mary Wollstonecraft | Biography, Beliefs, Books, A Vindication of the Rights of Woman, & Facts | Britannica
excerpts:
Section Title: Mary Wollstonecraft > *A Vindication of the Rights of Woman*
Content:
Mary Wollstonecraft's *A Vindication of the Rights of Woman* explained Learn about Mary Wollstonecraft's *A Vindication of the Rights of Woman* . (more)
See all videos for this article
The publication of *Vindication* caused considerable controversy but failed to bring about any immediate reforms. From the 1840s, however, members of the [incipient](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/incipient) American and European women’s movements resurrected some of the book’s principles. It was a particular influence on American women’s rights pioneers such as [Elizabeth Cady Stanton](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Elizabeth-Cady-Stanton) and [Margaret Fuller](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Margaret-Fuller) .

title: Mary Wollstonecraft - Wikipedia
excerpts: **Mary Wollstonecraft** ( / ˈ w ʊ l s t ən k r æ f t / , also UK : /- k r ɑː f t / ; <sup>[[ 1 ]]('s_Dictionaries-1)</sup> 27 April 1759 – 10 September 1797) was an English writer and philosopher best known for her advocacy of women's rights . <sup>[[ 2 ]]()</sup> <sup>[[ 3 ]]()</sup> Until the late twentieth century, Wollstonecraft's life, which encompassed several unconventional (at the time) personal relationships, received more attention than her writing. Wollstonecraft is regarded as one of the founding feminist philosophers , and feminists often cite both her life and her works as important influences.

title: Recent Work on the Reception History of Mary Wollstonecraft’s Philosophy | Analysis | Oxford Academic
excerpts:
Section Title: ... > 2. The context of Wollstonecraft’s reception
Content:
The recent interest in the reception of Wollstonecraft has in part been guided by a wish to refute the previous assumption that she was scandalized by the publication of her husband William Godwin’s *Memoirs of the Author of a Vindication of the Rights of Woman* (1798), which openly described her unconventional love life and suicide attempts. Her works were therefore soon forgotten, the assumption continues, with her *Vindication of the Rights of Woman* (1792) only being rediscovered by the nineteenth-century suffragist movement. Indeed, reception history can include the study of how a philosopher’s reputation was impacted by – and consequently had an impact on – the reaction to their ideas, and even how, and why, they were ‘forgotten’ and ‘recovered’ (see also Smith 2024 ; Taylor 2016 : 1–18).

title: Hannah Dawson on Mary Wollstonecraft - Philosophy Bites
excerpts:
Section Title: Hannah Dawson on Mary Wollstonecraft > TRANSCRIPT
Content:
**Guest:** Well, before The Vindication of the Rights of Woman, she had written a a number of works. She’d written a novel. She’d written a book about the education of daughters. And she actually made her name, first off, not with The Vindication of the Rights of Woman, but in fact, an earlier text, seventeen ninety, The Vindication of the Rights of Men. That was in response to Burke, to Edmund Burke, who’d written his reflections upon the revolution in France, where he’d criticized the French Revolution as this terrible event whereby the revolutionaries had tried to remake the world on the basis of reason and liberty, and they burned the house down in the process.
